notes: All 15 assertions pass for both with-skill and without-skill runs. The assertions are non-discriminating at this granularity — both configurations achieve the same pass rate.

The qualitative differences are substantial despite identical pass rates. The with-skill outputs consistently provide: (1) two revision options at varying levels of ambiguity instead of one, (2) more precise diagnostic vocabulary (e.g., 'fake simplicity,' 'emotional over-direction,' 'epistemic register'), (3) stronger framing of why revisions work, and (4) explicit connection to the earned-ambiguity philosophy.
The with-skill runs use ~43% more tokens and ~33% more time on average, reflecting the additional structure (dual revisions, deeper analysis).

The software-engineering eval shows the largest qualitative gap: with-skill explicitly names the fake simplicity pattern and frames configurability as a strength; without-skill is practical but more mechanical.
